analysis:
- Two clear design families emerge. High‑quality but expensive: exact greedy/local‑search (Heuristic 1, 2, 6) that repeatedly calls an exact hypervolume routine for marginal evaluations or uses population search. These give strong solutions but pay a very large CPU/HV cost inside inner loops (many repeated full HV computations).
- Best quality/runtime tradeoffs come from the MC‑assisted lazy‑greedy family (Heuristics 3–11). Their common, effective structure:
  - Pareto (nondominated) pruning to reduce N early.
  - A single shared Monte‑Carlo sample set to approximate coverage, computed with blockwise loops to control peak memory.
  - Representing per‑point coverage as boolean masks or compact index lists; using sparse index lists when avg domination is low dramatically reduces work.
  - Lazy greedy selection via a max‑heap with estimated gains and cheap incremental true‑gain recomputation (counting uncovered samples) — avoids recomputing full HV.
  - Simple tie‑breakers (box‑volume) and an inexpensive shortlist of promising swaps.
  - Bounded exact refinement: run exact hypervolume swap search only on a tiny shortlist (if pygmo present) or do mask‑based approximate swaps otherwise.
- Concrete efficiency patterns: blockwise mask construction and using index lists turn N×M boolean work into much cheaper integer counts or sparse accesses. Reusing masks for selection and polishing (rather than rebuilding) is key to speed.
- Avoidable overheads observed: recomputing exact HV in inner loops (Heuristic 1, parts of 2 and 6) and unbounded population growth (TPOSS) are the main cost drivers. Unbounded mask arrays N×M can be large — but most good designs handle this by blocking, capping M or using sparse lists. Several heuristics also benefit from fixed RNG seeds (deterministic behavior) and careful handling of degenerate reference/dimension ranges.
- Quality boosters with small cost: nondominated pruning, box‑volume tie‑breaks, deterministic sampling seeds, and a tiny (O(k)–O(5k)) shortlist for exact or approximate swap polishing produce consistent HV gains for marginal runtime increase.
experience: Prune dominated points, build one blockwise MC sample pool, and store per‑point coverage as boolean masks or sparse index lists. Use a lazy max‑heap (estimated gains → cheap true‑gain counts) to pick k, tie‑break with box‑volume, then run a bounded exact/approx swap polish on a small shortlist. Cap M, use block processing, and seed RNGs for reproducibility. Avoid repeated full HV calls inside inner loops.
current_reflection: Prune dominated points, build one blockwise MC sample pool, and store per‑point coverage as boolean masks or sparse index lists. Use a lazy max‑heap (estimated gains → cheap true‑gain counts) to pick k, tie‑break with box‑volume, then run a bounded exact/approx swap polish on a small shortlist. Cap M, use block processing, and seed RNGs for reproducibility. Avoid repeated full HV calls inside inner loops.
bad_reflections: Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, use a lazy greedy heap with incremental overlap updates, then polish only a small shortlist with exact HV. Cache hypervolume objects, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations. These give best quality per runtime.

Always prune dominated points, reuse one MC sample set and precomputed masks (use sparse lists when sparse), perform lazy greedy with incremental overlap updates, and limit exact HV calls to a small shortlisted pool with capped iterations. Tune sample count adaptively with k and D and block boolean work to be memory‑safe. Avoid recomputing hypervolume objects inside inner loops.

Prune dominated points, precompute shared MC samples and boolean masks in memory‑safe blocks, then run a lazy greedy heap with incremental covered masks. Use sparse index lists when appropriate, keep sample size proportional to k and D, and restrict exact HV calls to a tiny shortlisted pool for bounded swap polishing.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: dominated pruning, shared MC pool, boolean/sparse masks, lazy max‑heap, estimated gains, box‑volume tie‑break, bounded swap, block processing, seeded RNG
- Advice: Prune dominated points; build one blockwise MC sample pool; store per‑point coverage as boolean masks or sparse index lists; use a lazy max‑heap on estimated gains with cheap true‑gain updates and box‑volume tie‑breaks; shortlist top candidates and run a bounded exact/approx swap polish; cap M, seed RNGs, and cache hypervolume objects.
- Avoid: Recomputing full hypervolume or MC pools inside inner loops, full mask recomputation, unbounded refinements, and holding huge MC sets without block processing.
- Explanation: Reduces memory and runtime, enables fast incremental greedy selection with few exact HV calls, and yields reproducible bounded‑cost polishing.
